audit_items.proof_trace: `ber_skip_tag` (L269-279) unconditionally executes `ber->ber_ptr = bv.bv_val` and then `ber->ber_tag = *(unsigned char *) ber->ber_ptr` even when `ber_peek_element` returns LBER_DEFAULT. When the BER buffer is exhausted (`ber->ber_ptr == ber->ber_end`), the error path in `ber_tag_and_rest` sets `bv.bv_val = (char *) ber->ber_ptr = ber->ber_end`, so the subsequent deref reads one byte past the end of the allocated BER data buffer. This contrasts with `ber_skip_element` (L225-234) which guards the identical pattern with `if (tag != LBER_DEFAULT)`, confirming the missing guard is a defect rather than intentional design.
audit_items.attack_scenario: An attacker sends a malformed LDAP request where a BER-encoded sequence element terminates exactly at the buffer boundary; `ber_skip_tag` is invoked (e.g., via `ber_first_element` → `ber_scanf` → LDAP message parsing), `ber_peek_element` returns LBER_DEFAULT, and `*(unsigned char *) ber->ber_end` produces a 1-byte heap over-read that can crash the server or leak adjacent heap metadata.
